metadata.title: UK Energy Production, Imports and Self-Sufficiency
metadata.description: Annual UK energy data covering primary energy production, imports, oil & gas output, electricity mix, and self-sufficiency ratios

sources.name: DESNZ Digest of UK Energy Statistics (DUKES)
sources.notes: DUKES is the definitive annual UK energy publication. Chapter 1 provides aggregate energy balances in million tonnes of oil equivalent (Mtoe). Self-sufficiency = indigenous production / total inland energy consumption.

sources.name: DESNZ Energy Trends
sources.notes: Quarterly publication with latest electricity generation breakdown. Used for electricity mix data.

sources.name: DESNZ UK Energy in Brief
sources.notes: Annual summary publication. Cross-checks against DUKES main tables.

crossCheckNotes.selfSufficiency: DUKES defines self-sufficiency as indigenous production divided by inland energy consumption. This differs from import dependency (net imports / supply) because production can exceed consumption (exports). The UK was a net energy exporter from mid-1980s to 2004. Since 2004 the UK has been a net importer. DUKES and Energy in Brief use consistent definitions.
crossCheckNotes.oilGasProduction: DUKES Chapter 3 (oil) and Chapter 4 (gas) provide detailed production data. Oil is measured in million tonnes, gas in TWh or billion cubic metres. We convert to Mtoe for comparability using DUKES standard conversion factors. Cross-checked against NSTA (North Sea Transition Authority) production data which tracks UKCS output.
crossCheckNotes.electricityMix: DUKES Chapter 5 provides annual electricity data. Energy Trends provides quarterly updates. Both use the same underlying DESNZ data collection. Renewable percentage crossed 50% for the first time in 2024.

metadata.lastReleaseNotes: DESNZ Energy Trends 30 April 2026 release reviewed: highlights for Dec 2025-Feb 2026 vs prior year — primary energy consumption -3.3% (fuel input), -2.1% (temperature adjusted); indigenous production -4.5%; MPP electricity generation +1.4% with renewables +16%, gas -11%, nuclear -11%; renewables 51.8% MPP share, gas 35.5%, nuclear 11.9%; low-carbon share +5.0pp to 63.7%. Provisional sub-annual data — does not amend 2025 annual figures (DUKES revisions expected July 2026).

energyBalance.description: UK aggregate energy balance in million tonnes of oil equivalent (Mtoe)

series.notes: UK net energy exporter. North Sea peak era.
UK becomes net energy importer for first time since early 1980s.
Recession reduces energy demand.
Import dependency peaks above 50%.
COVID reduces demand. Self-sufficiency stable despite lower production.
Energy crisis. Gas prices spike. Minimal impact on self-sufficiency ratio.
Oil and gas production at record lows.
Production 68% below 1999 peak. Lowest self-sufficiency this century.
Total consumption 164.5 Mtoe, down 1.8% on 2024. Import dependency continues.

oilProduction.description: UK crude oil and NGL production
oilProduction.methodologyNote: Indigenous production of crude oil, natural gas liquids (NGLs) and feedstocks. Includes UKCS (UK Continental Shelf) offshore and onshore production.

series.notes: Near peak production.
Slight recovery from new field startups.
COVID impact.
Record low.
New record low. Down 42% on 2019.
Continued decline. Down 5.3% on 2024.

gasProduction.methodologyNote: Gross gas production from UKCS (offshore) and onshore fields. Includes associated gas from oil production.

series.notes: Near peak.
Temporary increase from new fields.
COVID impact on production.
Slight increase during energy crisis.
Record low.
New record low. Down 21% on 2019.
Production 332 TWh, down 3.3% on 2024. Record low.

electricityMix.description: UK electricity generation by source as percentage of total
electricityMix.methodologyNote: Shares of total UK electricity generation. Renewables includes wind, solar, hydro, and biomass. Fossil includes gas, coal, and oil. Imports are net electricity imports via interconnectors (France, Netherlands, Belgium, Norway, Denmark, Ireland).

series.notes: Coal collapses as gas + carbon price takes over.
COVID reduces demand. Renewables share jumps.
Low wind year. Gas fills the gap.
Renewables cross 50% for first time. Last coal plant closed Sept 2024. Record electricity imports.
Renewables hold above 50%. Last coal plant closed Oct 2024.
